mainEntity.name: The Dirty Tactics Insurance Companies Use to Deny Spinal Cord and Disc Claims
mainEntity.text: The Dirty Tactics Insurance Companies Use to Deny Spinal Cord and Disc Claims

acceptedAnswer.text:
The Dirty Tactics Insurance Companies Use to Deny Spinal Cord and Disc Claims

Working in heavy industries across Utah, Idaho, and Texas means back and neck injuries are a constant risk. Unfortunately, insurance companies often view these high-cost claims as a threat to their profits. Understanding their tactics is the best way to defend your rights.

The 'Degenerative' Defense

This is the number one tactic used in Salt Lake City and Boise for back and neck injuries. Because almost everyone has some spinal 'wear and tear' on an MRI after age 30, the insurance company will claim your pain is just 'getting older.' They ignore the fact that you were fine and working 40 hours a week until the accident happened. We fight this by showing that the work event was the 'proximate cause' of your current disability.

The 'Unwitnessed Accident' Skepticism

If you were working alone when you felt your back give way, the insurance company will often claim it never happened. They might look at your social media or talk to coworkers to try and find evidence that you actually hurt yourself at home. This is why having even a 'circumstantial' trail—like calling your spouse to complain about pain right after it happened or telling a coworker 'my back is killing me'—is so important.

Surveillance and Social Media

Insurance companies are not above hiring private investigators to follow you to the grocery store or watch you from a van outside your house in St. George. If they catch you doing something that looks 'normal,' like carrying a gallon of milk, they will use that footage at a hearing to argue that you are 'malingering' or faking your back injury. Always assume you are being filmed.

Independent Medical Exams (IME)

There is nothing 'independent' about an IME. These are doctors paid by the insurance company to provide a specific opinion—usually that you are healed or that your injury isn't work-related. If you are sent to an IME, you need to know exactly what to say and what not to say. We prep our clients for these exams so they don't fall into the doctor's traps.

The 'Lowball' Settlement Offer

Early in a claim, an adjuster might offer you $5,000 or $10,000 to 'close the case.' For many people struggling to pay bills, this sounds like a lot. However, if you have a herniated disc, a single surgery can cost $50,000, and your lost wages could be much higher. Once you sign a settlement, you can almost never go back for more. We help you calculate the *real* value of your injury before you even think about settling.

How Just Work Comp Law Can Help

Our firm was built to stand between injured workers and these predatory tactics. We provide elite legal representation and immediate action. Whether you are dealing with a denied claim in Utah County or a delayed surgery in Idaho, our attorneys—including Blair Jones and Mark Talley—know how to push back. You don't have to fight a billion-dollar insurance company alone. Call us today at 801-746-0290 for a free, transparent look at your case.
